test_cases.input: Climactic sequence of a political documentary: a key vote in a parliament. Available material includes observational lobby footage, interview excerpts from two politicians, archive of the vote announcement, and protest footage outside. Target: 7 minutes.
test_cases.notes: Scene ordering built appropriate tension through intercutting interior/exterior. Interview placement was correctly held until after the vote result was shown. Sequence Notes flagged the protest footage as risk (crowd may be unidentifiable).

test_cases.input: Mid-film expository sequence for a science documentary explaining how a virus mutates. Available: animation brief, expert interview, archived microscopy footage. Target: 4 minutes.
test_cases.notes: Correctly identified that expository sequences require visual support for every narration beat. Animation scenes were given specific function notes. B-roll requirements were technically specific for microscopy footage.

test_cases.input: Closing sequence for a personal documentary about a family reconciliation. Minimal available footage — one long observational scene at a family dinner and a final interview. Target: 5 minutes.
test_cases.notes: Handled sparse material well — built a viable scene structure from limited footage without inventing material that didn't exist. Sequence Notes was the strongest section, honestly assessing the risks of a sparse closing sequence.
